questions.optionB: Obstructive sleep apnea due to tonsillar hypertrophy (also: peritonsillar abscess, suspected malignancy, hemorrhagic tonsillitis)
questions.explanation: Core concept: OSA from tonsillar hypertrophy is an absolute indication for tonsillectomy. Why correct: Absolute indications: OSA from Grade 3-4 tonsillar hypertrophy, suspected malignancy (unilateral enlargement), peritonsillar abscess (interval tonsillectomy or à chaud), hemorrhagic tonsillitis. Relative: recurrent infections (Paradise criteria: ≥7 in 1 year, ≥5/year for 2 years, ≥3/year for 3 years). Why A wrong: <3/year doesn't meet Paradise criteria. Why C wrong: Single episode = not an indication. Why D wrong: Carrier state alone doesn't warrant surgery. Clinical relevance: Most common complication of tonsillectomy = hemorrhage (primary <24h or secondary 5-10 days). Post-tonsillectomy: referred otalgia (via CN IX) is common. Exam trap: Paradise criteria for recurrent tonsillitis = specific numbers (7-5-3 rule). Know these exactly. Memory aid: 'Tonsillectomy absolute = Obstruction (OSA) + suspicion of malignancy + abscess.' Reference: Dhingra – Pharynx.
